whatItMeasures: The 25-hydroxyvitamin D test measures the main circulating form of vitamin D in the blood, reflecting total vitamin D status from both sun exposure and dietary intake; vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption, bone health, and immune function.
causesOfHigh: Excessive vitamin D supplementation (most common cause of toxicity)
Granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, histoplasmosis — these convert vitamin D to its active form without normal regulation)
causesOfHighSource: Endocrine Society. Evaluation, Treatment, and Prevention of Vitamin D Deficiency. 2011. https://academic.oup.com/jcem/article/96/7/1911/2833671
causesOfLow: Insufficient sun exposure (key source of vitamin D)
Darker skin pigmentation (melanin reduces skin synthesis)
Older age (skin synthesis declines with age)
Obesity (vitamin D is fat-soluble and sequestered in adipose tissue)
Malabsorption disorders (celiac disease, Crohn's disease, cystic fibrosis)
Chronic kidney disease (impaired conversion to active vitamin D)
causesOfLowSource: Endocrine Society. Evaluation, Treatment, and Prevention of Vitamin D Deficiency. 2011. https://academic.oup.com/jcem/article/96/7/1911/2833671

high: Kidney stones (from hypercalcaemia caused by excess vitamin D)
Confusion and disorientation in severe cases
low: Bone pain and tenderness (particularly in adults: osteomalacia)
Frequent infections (vitamin D supports immune function)
In severe deficiency in children: rickets (bowed legs, soft skull bones)

citations.title: Evaluation, Treatment, and Prevention of Vitamin D Deficiency: an Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline
citations.source: Endocrine Society / Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism
